title: Reusable Design Patterns — Catalog
description: The vocabulary of system design. The case studies in this book keep reaching for the same handful of patterns; this catalog defines each one once — what it is, when to use it, and the trade-off it carries — so the cases can reference them by name. Grouped by the problem they solve.

patternCatalog.name: Horizontal sharding (partitioning)
patternCatalog.what: Split data across nodes by a partition key so no single node holds or serves the whole dataset. Also seen as "sharding by key", "partitioning by key", or "document partitioning".
patternCatalog.whenToUse: When data volume or write throughput exceeds one node's capacity.
patternCatalog.tradeoffs: Cross-shard operations (joins, transactions) become hard; the key choice determines everything. Failure mode to name: a hot shard — a key with skewed traffic (a celebrity, a popular product) overloads one node while the rest idle.

patternCatalog.name: Consistent hashing
patternCatalog.what: Map keys and nodes onto a ring so adding or removing a node only remaps a small fraction of keys. Use virtual nodes (many ring points per physical node) to smooth load.
patternCatalog.whenToUse: Distributing keys across a changing set of nodes (caches, shards) with minimal reshuffling.
patternCatalog.tradeoffs: Uneven load without virtual nodes; rebalancing still moves some data. Failure mode to name: without vnodes, removing one node dumps all its keys onto its single ring neighbour.

patternCatalog.name: Replication
patternCatalog.what: Keep copies of data on multiple nodes for availability, durability, and read scaling — leader-follower (one writer) or leaderless (write to many).
patternCatalog.whenToUse: When you need to survive node loss or serve more reads than one node can.
patternCatalog.tradeoffs: Replicas either lag (asynchronous, eventually consistent) or add write latency (synchronous); read-your-writes needs care. Failure mode to name: failing over to a stale follower that missed the last acknowledged writes loses committed data.

patternCatalog.name: Quorum reads/writes (N/W/R)
patternCatalog.what: Require R reads and W writes such that R + W > N to guarantee overlap with the latest write. Pair with read-repair and anti-entropy to converge replicas.
patternCatalog.whenToUse: Tuning the consistency/availability trade-off in a leaderless replicated store.
patternCatalog.tradeoffs: Higher R/W means stronger consistency but lower availability and higher latency. Failure mode to name: quorum overlap alone doesn't order concurrent writes — you still need versioning (vector clocks / last-writer-wins) to resolve conflicts.

patternCatalog.name: Consensus-replicated state (Raft/Paxos)
patternCatalog.what: Replicate a state machine through a consensus log so a majority agrees on every committed entry — giving a single, linearizable source of truth that survives a minority failure.
patternCatalog.whenToUse: Small, critical metadata that must be strongly consistent: locks, leader election, config, cluster membership, shard maps.
patternCatalog.tradeoffs: A write needs a majority round-trip, so throughput and latency are bounded — keep the replicated dataset small. Failure mode to name: a network partition leaves the minority side unable to make progress (CP, not AP).

patternCatalog.name: Geo / region partitioning
patternCatalog.what: Route and store each entity in a home region close to its users, with cross-region replication only where needed, so most traffic stays local.
patternCatalog.whenToUse: Global products with latency budgets, data-residency rules, or regional failure isolation.
patternCatalog.tradeoffs: Cross-region operations are slow and consistency gets hard; picking the partition geography (by user, by tenant, by data subject) is a one-way door. Failure mode to name: an entity that moves between regions, or traffic that spans regions, breaks the locality assumption.

patternCatalog.name: Caching (cache-aside)
patternCatalog.what: Read from cache first; on a miss, read the source of truth and backfill the cache. Variants: write-through / write-back, and TTL- or event-based invalidation.
patternCatalog.whenToUse: Read-heavy workloads with a hot subset of keys where slight staleness is acceptable.
patternCatalog.tradeoffs: Invalidation is the hard part; expect stale reads within the TTL. Failure mode to name: a thundering herd — a hot key expires and thousands of concurrent misses stampede the origin at once (mitigate with request coalescing or staggered TTLs).

patternCatalog.name: Read model (CQRS)
patternCatalog.what: Derive a fast, query-optimized projection from the authoritative write store instead of querying it directly. The projection is rebuildable from the source log, so it can be dropped and replayed.
patternCatalog.whenToUse: Read-heavy queries over a normalized or append-only write store that's expensive to aggregate live.
patternCatalog.tradeoffs: The read model lags the source and can have bugs — it must be rebuildable and never treated as the source of truth. Failure mode to name: a projector bug silently serves wrong data; you need a backfill/replay path and freshness monitoring to catch it.

patternCatalog.name: Indexing
patternCatalog.what: Maintain an auxiliary structure (B-tree, hash, inverted, geo) to answer queries without scanning the base data.
patternCatalog.whenToUse: Frequent lookups by an attribute that isn't the primary key.
patternCatalog.tradeoffs: Indexes cost write amplification and storage. Failure mode to name: a missing or stale index turns a fast lookup into a full scan that silently degrades latency under load.

patternCatalog.name: Fanout-on-write vs fanout-on-read
patternCatalog.what: Materialize a copy per consumer up front (write) or resolve consumers lazily at read/send time (read). A hybrid pulls celebrity/large-audience content at read time and fans the rest out on write.
patternCatalog.whenToUse: Feed and notification delivery; choose by audience size and update frequency.
patternCatalog.tradeoffs: Write fanout is fast to read but expensive for huge audiences; read fanout is the reverse. Failure mode to name: a celebrity with millions of followers makes pure write-fanout explode — the reason real feeds go hybrid.

patternCatalog.name: Change data capture (CDC) / change streams
patternCatalog.what: Stream a database's committed row changes (from its replication log) as an event feed that downstream consumers project into caches, search indexes, and read models.
patternCatalog.whenToUse: Keeping derived stores in sync with a source DB without dual-writes, and decoupling read paths from the write schema.
patternCatalog.tradeoffs: Adds a pipeline to operate and consumers see changes after a lag; ordering is per-partition, not global. Failure mode to name: a dual-write (app writes DB and queue separately) drifts on partial failure — CDC exists precisely to avoid that; pair with the outbox when you need business events, not raw row diffs.
aliases: Async index update via change stream

patternCatalog.name: Request coalescing (anti-stampede)
patternCatalog.what: Collapse concurrent identical misses into a single in-flight origin request whose result fans back out to all waiters.
patternCatalog.whenToUse: Hot keys behind a cache or expensive computation where many clients ask for the same thing at once.
patternCatalog.tradeoffs: Adds an in-flight registry and a shared wait; one slow origin call now stalls every coalesced waiter. Failure mode to name: without it, a single expired hot key produces a thundering herd that takes down the origin.

patternCatalog.name: Work queue
patternCatalog.what: Decouple producers from consumers with a durable queue and pull-based workers, using a visibility-timeout / ack so an unfinished message is redelivered.
patternCatalog.whenToUse: When producers are fast/bursty but downstream work is slow or rate-limited.
patternCatalog.tradeoffs: Adds a queue to operate (watch depth and consumer lag); delivery is at-least-once, so consumers must be idempotent. Failure mode to name: a worker that crashes after doing the work but before acking causes a duplicate redelivery.

patternCatalog.name: Transactional outbox
patternCatalog.what: Write events to an outbox table in the same transaction as the state change, then a relay (polling or CDC) ships them to a queue and marks them done.
patternCatalog.whenToUse: Publishing an event reliably alongside a DB write without distributed transactions.
patternCatalog.tradeoffs: Adds a relay process and outbox cleanup; events are at-least-once, so consumers dedupe. Failure mode to name: a relay crash between publish and mark-done re-emits the event — ordering and idempotency on the consumer are mandatory, not optional.

patternCatalog.name: Saga / state machine
patternCatalog.what: Model a multi-step workflow as explicit, persisted states with compensating actions on failure, instead of one distributed transaction. Each transition is idempotent and replayable.
patternCatalog.whenToUse: Long-running flows spanning external systems that can fail partway (orders, payments, bookings).
patternCatalog.tradeoffs: Compensation logic is complex and intermediate states are user-visible. Failure mode to name: a flow stuck in a non-terminal state (provider never replied) needs a reconciliation/sweeper to time it out and compensate, or it leaks resources forever.

patternCatalog.name: Dead-letter queue
patternCatalog.what: Route messages that exhaust retries to a separate queue for inspection instead of dropping them or blocking the main queue head.
patternCatalog.whenToUse: Any retrying consumer where some messages are permanently bad (poison messages).
patternCatalog.tradeoffs: Requires monitoring and a replay/repair process. Failure mode to name: an unwatched DLQ silently swallows real failures — DLQ depth must page someone, and replays must be idempotent.

patternCatalog.name: Backpressure
patternCatalog.what: Let a slow consumer push back on producers — bounded queues, admission control, load shedding, priority isolation, or a downstream rate limit — rather than collapse.
patternCatalog.whenToUse: Any pipeline where producers can outpace consumers.
patternCatalog.tradeoffs: Someone must absorb the slack (buffer, shed, or reject), and each choice trades latency for loss differently. Failure mode to name: unbounded buffering doesn't prevent failure, it just defers it into an out-of-memory crash with a huge backlog.

patternCatalog.name: Retry with backoff + jitter
patternCatalog.what: On a transient failure, retry after an exponentially growing delay with random jitter, capped by a max attempt count or deadline.
patternCatalog.whenToUse: Any call to a flaky dependency where the failure might clear on its own.
patternCatalog.tradeoffs: Retries multiply load exactly when a dependency is struggling, and only safe operations may be retried. Failure mode to name: synchronized retries with no jitter create a retry storm / thundering herd; pair with a circuit breaker and make the target idempotent.

patternCatalog.name: Persistent connections (WebSocket / SSE)
patternCatalog.what: Hold a long-lived connection so the server can push to the client (and the client stream up) without polling — WebSocket for bidirectional, SSE for server-to-client.
patternCatalog.whenToUse: Real-time delivery: chat, presence, live feeds, collaborative editing, streaming LLM tokens.
patternCatalog.tradeoffs: Connections are stateful, so you need a connection registry and routing to reach a given user, plus reconnect/resume handling. Failure mode to name: a mass reconnect (deploy, network blip) stampedes the gateway — stagger with jittered reconnect and admission control.

patternCatalog.name: Idempotency key
patternCatalog.what: A client-supplied key the server stores with the result, so a retried request replays the original response instead of re-executing the effect. Define the key scope, a retention window, and how an in-flight duplicate is handled.
patternCatalog.whenToUse: Any state-changing API over an unreliable network where clients retry — especially money and notifications.
patternCatalog.tradeoffs: Needs a dedup store with a defined TTL, and clients must generate and reuse the key correctly. Failure mode to name: two concurrent requests with the same key race — you must lock the key or atomically claim it, or the effect runs twice.

patternCatalog.name: Deduplication
patternCatalog.what: Collapse duplicate events within a window — keyed on a stable event/message id in a dedup store — so downstream effects happen once. (The server-side complement to an idempotency key.)
patternCatalog.whenToUse: At-least-once pipelines, duplicate user actions, or repeated external events.
patternCatalog.tradeoffs: Window sizing trades storage against correctness. Failure mode to name: a window too short treats a legitimate retry arriving late as a new event (double effect); too long, and the dedup store grows unbounded.

patternCatalog.name: Double-entry ledger
patternCatalog.what: Record every money movement as balanced debit/credit entries that sum to zero, append-only and immutable; never mutate or delete an entry — correct with a reversing entry. Serve balances from a materialized view.
patternCatalog.whenToUse: Whenever correctness and auditability of balances matter more than write convenience.
patternCatalog.tradeoffs: More writes per transaction and balances must be derived, not stored. Failure mode to name: caching a balance that drifts from the entries — the ledger is the truth, the balance view is rebuildable and must reconcile to it.

patternCatalog.name: Reconciliation
patternCatalog.what: Periodically compare internal records against an authoritative source on a matching key, then repair drift with explicit correction records, an audit trail, and an operator workflow for breaks.
patternCatalog.whenToUse: Any integration where a second system also holds the truth (PSPs, banks, providers), or where stuck workflow states need sweeping.
patternCatalog.tradeoffs: Break volume grows with scale and needs ops tooling. Failure mode to name: silently auto-correcting toward the wrong source — name which side is authoritative before writing any correction.

patternCatalog.name: Leases & fencing tokens
patternCatalog.what: Grant time-bounded exclusive access (a lease, refreshed by heartbeat) and attach a monotonic fencing token; the protected resource must reject any token older than the highest it has seen.
patternCatalog.whenToUse: Leader election, distributed locks, single-writer guarantees, seat/inventory holds.
patternCatalog.tradeoffs: Clock and GC-pause hazards make the lease alone insufficient. Failure mode to name: a paused holder wakes after its lease expired and writes with a stale token — only resource-side token rejection (not the lease) actually prevents the split-brain write.

patternCatalog.name: Optimistic concurrency control (CAS)
patternCatalog.what: Read a row with its version, and on write require the version to be unchanged (compare-and-set); if it changed, reject and let the caller re-read and retry.
patternCatalog.whenToUse: Low-contention updates where a pessimistic lock would be overkill — profile edits, counters, config, cart updates.
patternCatalog.tradeoffs: Cheap when conflicts are rare, but a hot row turns every write into a retry loop. Failure mode to name: under high contention CAS degrades to livelock; switch to a queue/serialized writer or a hold/lock for hot keys.

patternCatalog.name: Inventory hold / reservation
patternCatalog.what: Atomically claim a scarce unit (seat, room-night, stock) with a short TTL hold, then confirm or let it auto-expire (reap) so abandoned attempts release the unit.
patternCatalog.whenToUse: High-contention booking and checkout where you must not oversell.
patternCatalog.tradeoffs: Holds add a reservation state and a reaper to operate, and the TTL trades conversion against inventory lockup. Failure mode to name: a confirm that races a hold expiry — make confirm idempotent and check the hold is still valid, or you double-book.

patternCatalog.name: Rate limiting / throttling
patternCatalog.what: Cap requests per key (token / leaky bucket) to protect resources and enforce fairness across tenants.
patternCatalog.whenToUse: Public APIs, multi-tenant systems, and protecting rate-limited downstreams.
patternCatalog.tradeoffs: Distributed counters need a fast shared store, and the limit trades UX against abuse. Failure mode to name: a per-node limiter under-counts in aggregate (each node sees a fraction of traffic) — you need a shared counter or a sketch to enforce a global limit.

patternCatalog.name: Sharded / priority queues
patternCatalog.what: Partition a queue by tenant/key and separate priority lanes so one tenant or a bulk load can't starve others — fair scheduling over per-tenant partitions.
patternCatalog.whenToUse: Multi-tenant async pipelines with mixed urgent and bulk traffic.
patternCatalog.tradeoffs: More partitions and lanes to operate, and the routing key must actually isolate tenants. Failure mode to name: a strict priority lane starves the low-priority lane indefinitely — use weighted fair scheduling, not absolute priority.

patternCatalog.name: Load shedding / admission control
patternCatalog.what: When overloaded, reject or degrade lower-value work at the edge before it enters the system, so accepted work still meets its SLO.
patternCatalog.whenToUse: Spiky traffic that can exceed capacity: launches, flash sales, viral events, dependency brownouts.
patternCatalog.tradeoffs: You must classify work by value and decide what to drop first, and a rejected user sees an error. Failure mode to name: admitting everything and degrading uniformly means total collapse — partial availability beats whole-system failure.

patternCatalog.name: Circuit breaker
patternCatalog.what: Track a dependency's failure rate; once it crosses a threshold, open the circuit and fail fast (or serve a fallback) instead of piling calls onto a sick service, then probe to re-close.
patternCatalog.whenToUse: Calls to a dependency that can become slow or unavailable, especially behind retries.
patternCatalog.tradeoffs: An open breaker turns away requests that might have succeeded, and the thresholds need tuning. Failure mode to name: retries without a breaker turn a slow dependency into a full outage as queued calls and timeouts cascade upstream.

patternCatalog.name: Virtual waiting room
patternCatalog.what: Put a token-gated queue in front of a scarce resource so users are admitted at a controlled rate with a fair, visible position instead of a stampede.
patternCatalog.whenToUse: Demand that vastly exceeds capacity for a short window: ticket on-sales, limited drops, flash sales.
patternCatalog.tradeoffs: Adds a queueing tier and a fairness policy to operate, and a waiting user is a held connection. Failure mode to name: the waiting room itself becomes the bottleneck (or can be gamed) if entry isn't admission-controlled and tokens aren't bound to identity.

patternCatalog.name: Geo-indexing (geohash / S2)
patternCatalog.what: Encode locations (geohash, S2, quadtree) so nearby points are queryable by prefix or range; a radius query expands to the cell plus its neighbours. Adapt cell size to density.
patternCatalog.whenToUse: Proximity search, ride matching, nearby-friends, mapping.
patternCatalog.tradeoffs: Cell-boundary cases mean two close points can land in different cells. Failure mode to name: a hot cell in a dense area (downtown at rush hour) concentrates load — you need neighbour expansion plus adaptive cell sizing, not a fixed grid.

patternCatalog.name: Inverted index
patternCatalog.what: Map each term to a posting list of documents that contain it, so a text query intersects posting lists instead of scanning documents. Partition by document (scatter-gather) at scale.
patternCatalog.whenToUse: Full-text search, log search, autocomplete, faceted catalog search.
patternCatalog.tradeoffs: Index build/update lags writes, and analysis (tokenization, stemming) is a one-way commitment baked into the index. Failure mode to name: the index drifts from the source — rebuild it from an authoritative log with tombstones for deletes, never treat it as the system of record.
aliases: Source-of-truth + projected search index with tombstones

patternCatalog.name: Vector index (ANN)
patternCatalog.what: Embed items as vectors and use approximate nearest-neighbour search (HNSW, IVF) to retrieve semantically similar items in sublinear time.
patternCatalog.whenToUse: Semantic search, recommendations, RAG retrieval, image/visual similarity, fuzzy dedup.
patternCatalog.tradeoffs: ANN trades recall for speed, and embeddings must stay consistent between index time and query time. Failure mode to name: re-embedding with a new model invalidates the whole index — you must reindex, and mixed-model vectors silently return garbage neighbours.

patternCatalog.name: Candidate generation + two-stage ranking
patternCatalog.what: Cheaply retrieve a large candidate set (recall), then apply a heavier model to score and re-rank a small top-k (precision), with post-filters for diversity, freshness, and policy.
patternCatalog.whenToUse: Feeds, search, recommendations, and ads where scoring everything is infeasible.
patternCatalog.tradeoffs: Two stages add a pipeline and a recall/precision tuning surface. Failure mode to name: anything the cheap recall stage misses can never be ranked — recall sets the ceiling, so the candidate generator, not the ranker, is usually the real bottleneck.

patternCatalog.name: Time-series storage + rollups
patternCatalog.what: Store metrics/events in time-bucketed, columnar, compressed chunks; pre-aggregate (downsample) into coarser rollups and tier old data to cheap storage.
patternCatalog.whenToUse: Monitoring, analytics, billing, and any append-heavy data queried by time range.
patternCatalog.tradeoffs: Rollups commit you to fixed aggregation windows and lose raw fidelity, and high label cardinality blows up the index. Failure mode to name: unbounded label/series cardinality (a user-id label) explodes storage and query cost — control cardinality at ingest.

patternCatalog.name: Provider abstraction / adapter
patternCatalog.what: A uniform interface behind per-provider adapters so downstreams can be added, swapped, or failed over with health-aware routing.
patternCatalog.whenToUse: Multiple interchangeable external dependencies (payment PSPs, push/email/SMS vendors, CDNs).
patternCatalog.tradeoffs: The abstraction leaks provider-specific quirks, and health-aware routing adds complexity. Failure mode to name: a lowest-common-denominator interface that hides a provider capability you actually needed, forcing per-provider escape hatches that defeat the abstraction.

patternCatalog.name: Synchronous request/response
patternCatalog.what: The caller blocks for a result within one request — the simplest interaction model, fronted by load balancing across stateless servers.
patternCatalog.whenToUse: Reads and short writes where the client needs an answer now and the work fits inside a request budget.
patternCatalog.tradeoffs: Caller availability and latency are coupled to the callee's, and long work doesn't fit. Failure mode to name: doing slow work (fanout, transcode, third-party call) inline blocks the request and exhausts the connection pool — push it async behind a queue and return a status.

patternCatalog.name: API gateway
patternCatalog.what: A single entry tier that terminates TLS, authenticates, rate-limits, routes, and aggregates — keeping cross-cutting concerns out of every backend service.
patternCatalog.whenToUse: Any multi-service backend with shared auth, throttling, and routing needs.
patternCatalog.tradeoffs: It's a shared chokepoint that must scale and stay highly available, and logic creep turns it into a monolith. Failure mode to name: the gateway becomes a single point of failure or a deploy bottleneck — keep it thin and horizontally scalable.

patternCatalog.name: CDN / edge caching
patternCatalog.what: Serve static and cacheable content from points of presence near users, with a tiered origin shield to absorb misses, so the origin handles a fraction of traffic.
patternCatalog.whenToUse: Static assets, media segments, and cacheable API responses with global reach.
patternCatalog.tradeoffs: Edge invalidation is slow and eventual, and dynamic/personalized content can't be cached naively. Failure mode to name: a stale-after-deploy asset or an unprimed cache causes a cold-cache origin stampede on launch — version URLs and pre-warm.

patternCatalog.name: RBAC / ABAC authorization
patternCatalog.what: Decide access from roles (RBAC) or from attributes of subject, resource, and context (ABAC), evaluated by a central policy engine and cached per decision with versioning.
patternCatalog.whenToUse: Most app authorization: who may do what, with tenant, environment, or time conditions.
patternCatalog.tradeoffs: RBAC role explosion vs. ABAC policy complexity, and caching decisions risks serving stale grants. Failure mode to name: a revoked permission that a cached decision keeps honoring — bound cache TTL and version it against policy changes.

patternCatalog.name: ReBAC (relationship tuples)
patternCatalog.what: Model authorization as a graph of relationship tuples (user→relation→object) and answer "can X do Y on Z?" by traversing it — the Zanzibar approach — with consistency tokens (zookies) to avoid stale checks.
patternCatalog.whenToUse: Fine-grained, hierarchical sharing: documents, folders, nested groups, org charts.
patternCatalog.tradeoffs: Graph checks can be deep and latency-sensitive, and you must cache without serving stale permissions. Failure mode to name: a check that reads a replica lagging behind a recent permission change — consistency tokens pin the check to a known-fresh snapshot.

patternCatalog.name: OAuth / OIDC + token lifecycle
patternCatalog.what: Federate identity via OAuth/OIDC and issue short-lived access tokens with rotating refresh tokens; revoke via short TTLs or a denylist and rotate signing keys (JWKS).
patternCatalog.whenToUse: Login, third-party delegated access, and service-to-service auth.
patternCatalog.tradeoffs: Stateless JWTs are fast to verify but hard to revoke before expiry; stateful sessions revoke instantly but need a lookup. Failure mode to name: a long-lived or non-revocable access token stays valid after logout/compromise — keep access TTLs short and make revocation real.

patternCatalog.name: Permission-scoped search
patternCatalog.what: Filter search results by what the caller is allowed to see — either pre-filtering the index by ACL or post-filtering ranked results against the authorization service.
patternCatalog.whenToUse: Search over access-controlled content: workspaces, files, messages, tickets.
patternCatalog.tradeoffs: Pre-filtering bloats the index with ACLs that change often; post-filtering can return empty pages after filtering. Failure mode to name: an index whose ACLs lag a permission revocation leaks results the user no longer may see — revalidate sensitive hits at read time.

patternCatalog.name: Multipart / resumable upload
patternCatalog.what: Split a large object into parts uploaded independently (and retryably), then commit metadata last so a partial upload never looks complete.
patternCatalog.whenToUse: Large file and media uploads over unreliable networks.
patternCatalog.tradeoffs: Adds part tracking, a commit step, and GC of abandoned parts. Failure mode to name: committing metadata before all parts land exposes a half-written object — commit-metadata-last, and reap orphaned parts on a TTL.

patternCatalog.name: Content-addressed chunking
patternCatalog.what: Split data into chunks keyed by a hash of their content, so identical chunks are stored once (dedup) and a sync only transfers changed chunks (delta sync).
patternCatalog.whenToUse: File sync, backup, and deduplicated storage with overlapping or incrementally-edited data.
patternCatalog.tradeoffs: Chunk indexing and reference-counted GC add bookkeeping, and chunk-size choice trades dedup ratio against overhead. Failure mode to name: deleting a chunk still referenced by another object corrupts it — GC must be reference-counted, not time-based.

patternCatalog.name: Metadata / blob split
patternCatalog.what: Keep small mutable metadata in a database and large immutable bytes in object storage / CDN, linking them by id, so each tier scales on its own access pattern.
patternCatalog.whenToUse: Anything with large payloads and small queryable attributes: media, documents, attachments.
patternCatalog.tradeoffs: Two stores can drift, and a write spans both. Failure mode to name: metadata that points at a blob that was never written (or vice-versa) — order the writes (blob first, commit metadata last) and reconcile orphans.

patternCatalog.name: Erasure coding
patternCatalog.what: Split an object into k data + m parity fragments across nodes so any k of k+m reconstruct it — durability close to replication at a fraction of the storage cost.
patternCatalog.whenToUse: Cold/warm large-object storage where replication's 3× overhead is too expensive.
patternCatalog.tradeoffs: Reads and especially repairs cost CPU and cross-node I/O, and small-object overhead is high. Failure mode to name: a slow/expensive reconstruct on the read path — tier by object class (replicate hot, erasure-code cold), don't erasure-code everything.

patternCatalog.name: Adaptive bitrate streaming
patternCatalog.what: Transcode media into a ladder of bitrates split into short segments (HLS/DASH); the player switches rungs per segment to match bandwidth, served from a CDN.
patternCatalog.whenToUse: Video and audio streaming across varied devices and networks.
patternCatalog.tradeoffs: Transcoding the ladder is expensive compute and storage, and DRM/entitlement gates each segment. Failure mode to name: too coarse a ladder causes rebuffering or quality cliffs — QoE telemetry must drive ladder and CDN-steering decisions.

patternCatalog.name: Durable change log + idempotent projectors
patternCatalog.what: Treat an append-only, replayable log (Kafka-style) as the source of order, and build every derived store by replaying it through idempotent projectors that can be re-run from any offset.
patternCatalog.whenToUse: Event-sourced systems and any pipeline that must rebuild read models, caches, or search indexes deterministically from history.
patternCatalog.tradeoffs: Projectors must be idempotent and replay-safe, and a wide replay is slow. Failure mode to name: a projector bug corrupts a derived store silently — you fix the projector and replay from a known-good offset, so the log (not the projection) must be the retained source of truth.

patternCatalog.name: Consumer groups + offsets
patternCatalog.what: Partition a log and let a group of consumers each own a subset of partitions, committing a per-partition offset so progress survives restarts and the group rebalances when a member joins or leaves.
patternCatalog.whenToUse: Scaling consumption of a partitioned log horizontally while keeping per-partition ordering and at-least-once progress.
patternCatalog.tradeoffs: Ordering holds only within a partition, and a rebalance pauses consumption (and can redeliver from the last committed offset). Failure mode to name: committing the offset before the work finishes loses messages on a crash; committing after redelivers — pick at-least-once and make consumers idempotent.

patternCatalog.name: Event versioning + ordered apply
patternCatalog.what: Stamp each event with a monotonic source version (or sequence) and have consumers apply only newer versions, so out-of-order or duplicate delivery can't regress state.
patternCatalog.whenToUse: At-least-once streams where events for the same entity can arrive late, retried, or reordered across partitions.
patternCatalog.tradeoffs: Consumers must persist a per-entity high-water mark and reject stale events. Failure mode to name: applying a stale event over a newer one silently rolls state backward — compare-and-apply on the version, don't blindly upsert.

patternCatalog.name: Conflict-free replication (CRDT / OT)
patternCatalog.what: Let multiple replicas accept concurrent edits and converge without a central lock — CRDTs merge commutatively, Operational Transformation rewrites concurrent ops against a server-ordered log. Pair with a snapshot plus an op log for compaction.
patternCatalog.whenToUse: Real-time collaborative editing, offline-first apps, and multi-device sync where users edit the same state at once.
patternCatalog.tradeoffs: CRDT metadata grows and OT transform functions are notoriously hard to get right; both demand careful intention-preservation. Failure mode to name: an incorrect merge/transform silently diverges replicas — you need a server-ordered op log (or convergence proof) and a snapshot+op-log path to recover and compact.

patternCatalog.name: Feature store (online/offline parity)
patternCatalog.what: Compute features once and serve them from a low-latency online store for inference and a batch offline store for training, guaranteeing the same transformation produces both so training and serving see identical values.
patternCatalog.whenToUse: ML systems where the same features power model training and real-time inference (recommendations, fraud, ranking).
patternCatalog.tradeoffs: Two stores and a shared compute path add pipeline complexity, and freshness vs. cost must be tuned per feature. Failure mode to name: train/serve skew — the offline and online paths compute a feature differently, so the model sees one distribution in training and another in production; enforce a single transformation and monitor drift.

patternCatalog.name: Model routing / cascading + inference batching
patternCatalog.what: Route each request to the cheapest model that can handle it (escalating to a heavier one only when needed) and batch concurrent inferences to amortize accelerator cost, with a safety/guardrail gate around the model.
patternCatalog.whenToUse: LLM and ML serving where latency, accuracy, and GPU cost must be balanced across heterogeneous traffic.
patternCatalog.tradeoffs: Cascades add routing logic and a quality/cost tuning surface, and batching trades per-request latency for throughput. Failure mode to name: an over-eager cheap tier silently degrades answer quality, or aggressive batching blows the latency SLO — route on a measured confidence/complexity signal and cap batch wait time.

patternCatalog.name: Lakehouse ELT (governed raw + warehouse transform)
patternCatalog.what: Land raw source data immutably in a governed lake, then transform inside the warehouse (ELT, not ETL) so transformations are versioned SQL you can re-run, with a schema registry tracking evolution and idempotent partitioned loads.
patternCatalog.whenToUse: Analytics and data-warehouse pipelines that must reprocess history, audit lineage, and absorb upstream schema changes.
patternCatalog.tradeoffs: Raw-lake storage grows and transform jobs must stay idempotent and partition-scoped. Failure mode to name: a non-idempotent load double-counts on retry, or a silent upstream schema change breaks downstream tables — register schemas, evolve them explicitly, and make loads re-runnable on a partition.

patternCatalog.name: Progressive delivery (release safety)
patternCatalog.what: Ship change behind staged rollout — feature flags, canaries, and percentage ramps with automated rollback on regression — gated by immutable, signed artifacts and GitOps-declared desired state, behind a merge queue that only lands changes that pass on the integration branch.
patternCatalog.whenToUse: Continuous delivery of services, web, and mobile where a bad change must be contained and reverted fast.
patternCatalog.tradeoffs: Flag and rollout machinery adds operational surface and flag debt, and signing/GitOps add release-pipeline steps. Failure mode to name: a flag that ramps without a real health signal (or never gets cleaned up) becomes a silent landmine — wire rollout to SLO-based automated rollback and retire stale flags.

followUps: For a given case, which two or three of these patterns dominate the design, and why?
Which patterns compose into one pipeline (outbox → work queue → idempotency/dedup → DLQ; sharding → replication → quorum; lease → fencing → single writer), and which conflict?
For each pattern, what is the single failure mode an interviewer most wants you to name?
Which pairs are commonly confused — idempotency key vs. dedup, optimistic concurrency vs. a lock, CDC vs. outbox, fanout-on-write vs. -on-read — and when does each win?

links.groupDescription: Core system design references for scalability, reliability, and operating distributed services.
links.title: AWS Well-Architected Framework
links.why: A practical baseline for availability, reliability, operations, and scaling tradeoffs.

links.groupDescription: Core system design references for scalability, reliability, and operating distributed services.
links.title: Designing Data-Intensive Applications
links.why: A broad reference for replication, partitioning, logs, transactions, and stream processing tradeoffs.

links.groupDescription: Core system design references for scalability, reliability, and operating distributed services.
links.title: Monitoring Distributed Systems
links.why: Grounds monitoring and alerting decisions in user-visible reliability signals.

links.groupDescription: Core system design references for scalability, reliability, and operating distributed services.
links.title: Handling Overload
links.why: Useful for admission control, load shedding, quotas, and graceful degradation under spikes.

links.groupDescription: Core system design references for scalability, reliability, and operating distributed services.
links.title: The Tail at Scale
links.why: Explains why p99 latency dominates large fanout systems and motivates hedging, caching, and bounded work.

links.groupDescription: Primary papers and docs for storage, replication, partitioning, consistency, and caching.
links.title: Dynamo: Amazon's Highly Available Key-value Store
links.why: Canonical production design for partitioning, replication, hinted handoff, vector clocks, and tunable consistency.

links.groupDescription: Messaging, event streaming, delivery semantics, and async processing references.
links.title: Apache Kafka Design
links.why: Explains Kafka's log, partitions, consumer groups, replication, and operational model.

links.groupDescription: Authentication, authorization, policy, abuse, and identity standards.
links.title: Zanzibar: Google's Consistent, Global Authorization System
links.why: Canonical relationship-based authorization system with consistency tokens and global checks.

links.groupDescription: Authentication, authorization, policy, abuse, and identity standards.
links.title: OpenID Connect Core 1.0
links.why: The standard for federated login and token issuance layered on OAuth 2.0 — grounds the token-lifecycle and revocation patterns.

links.groupDescription: Metrics, tracing, logs, analytics, data pipelines, warehouses, and orchestration references.
links.title: Prometheus Overview
links.why: Concrete time-series monitoring architecture and data model — grounds the time-series storage and rollup patterns.

links.groupDescription: Metrics, tracing, logs, analytics, data pipelines, warehouses, and orchestration references.
links.title: OpenTelemetry Documentation
links.why: Vendor-neutral standard for metrics, traces, and logs — the instrumentation layer behind monitoring, sampling, and admission-control signals.
